turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm not sure where to start. I've tried quitting so many times before, but I always end up relapsing. I'm just really discouraged, I guess. I feel like I've made progress, but I'm scared I'll fall back into old habits.

step1_extraction.motivation_reason: discouraged, scared of falling back into old habits

step2_rag_chunks: Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

motivation_reason: discouraged, scared of falling back into old habits

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the ASSESSMENT phase. Focus on gathering basic facts: how long they have been smoking, how much they smoke per day, and what substance. Do NOT ask about triggers or suggest strategies yet. Ask only one simple factual question.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

turns.user_message: Well, I had a health scare a few months ago, and it really made me realize how much I'm putting my health at risk. Plus, my kids have been on my case about quitting, and I want to set a good example for them. But, to be honest, it's hard for me to imagine my daily routine without cigarettes. I've always smoked during my morning coffee, after lunch, and during work breaks. I'm not sure how to fill those gaps with healthier alternatives.
step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.
Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.

motivation_reason: discouraged, scared of falling back into old habits

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Honestly, I'm not sure where to start. I've tried quitting so many times before, but I always end up relapsing. I'm just really discouraged, I guess. I feel like I've made progress, but I'm scared I'll fall back into old habits.
Therapist: I completely understand, Rachel. It takes a lot of courage to keep trying. Can you tell me more about what's been different this time around? What made you want to quit again?

I've always smoked during my morning coffee, after lunch, and during work breaks. I'm not sure how to fill those gaps with healthier alternatives.

[Recent Conversation]
Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Honestly, I'm not sure where to start. I've tried quitting so many times before, but I always end up relapsing. I'm just really discouraged, I guess. I feel like I've made progress, but I'm scared I'll fall back into old habits.
Therapist: I completely understand, Rachel. It takes a lot of courage to keep trying. Can you tell me more about what's been different this time around? What made you want to quit again?

Patient: Well, I had a health scare a few months ago, and it really made me realize how much I'm putting my health at risk. Plus, my kids have been on my case about quitting, and I want to set a good example for them. But, to be honest, it's hard for me to imagine my daily routine without cigarettes. I've always smoked during my morning coffee, after lunch, and during work breaks. I'm not sure how to fill those gaps with healthier alternatives.

Therapist: I see. It sounds like you're struggling with the habit and routine aspect of quitting. That can be really tough. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you didn't have cigarettes during those times? What do you think you would miss most about smoking?

Patient: I think I would miss the feeling of taking a break, you know? Smoking has always been my way of taking a few minutes for myself, especially during stressful times at work. And, I guess I would miss the social aspect of it too. My friends and I always smoke together when we're out. But, deep down, I know it's not good for me, and I want to find healthier ways to cope with stress and boredom.
